title: Pointwise Generalization in Deep Neural Networks
abstract: We address the fundamental question of why deep neural networks generalize by establishing a pointwise generalization theory for fully connected networks. This framework resolves long-standing barriers to characterizing the rich nonlinear feature-learning regime and builds a new statistical foundation for representation learning. For each trained model, we characterize the hypothesis via a pointwise Riemannian Dimension, derived from the eigenvalues of the learned feature representations across layers. This establishes a principled framework for deriving hypothesis-dependent, representation-aware generalization bounds. These bounds offer a systematic upgrade over approaches based on model size, products of norms, and infinite-width linearizations, yielding guarantees that are orders of magnitude tighter in both theory and experiment. Analytically, we identify the structural properties and mathematical principles that explain the tractability of deep networks. Empirically, the pointwise Riemannian Dimension exhibits substantial feature compression, decreases with increased over-parameterization, and captures the implicit bias of optimizers. Taken together, our results indicate that deep networks are mathematically tractable in practical regimes and that their generalization is sharply explained by pointwise, feature-spectrum-aware complexity.
summary: The paper proposes a new theory of generalization for deep learning that moves away from worst-case global complexity to a localized, feature-dependent measure. By focusing on the effective dimension of learned representations, it explains how overparameterization actually aids generalization through feature compression. This provides a much tighter mathematical bound on the performance of neural networks than traditional methods.
main_problem: Explaining why massive, overparameterized deep neural networks generalize well in practice despite classical theory predicting severe overfitting.
main_result: Established a pointwise generalization theory where the generalization gap is controlled by a 'Riemannian Dimension' derived from the eigenvalues of learned feature representations, providing bounds orders of magnitude tighter than previous methods.
method: A non-perturbative, representation-aware framework using hierarchical covering on Grassmannian manifolds and a mixed empirical-ghost metric to analyze the geometry of the feature space.
model_or_system: Fully connected (feed-forward) Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) with L layers, widths d_l, and 1-Lipschitz nonlinear activations like ReLU.
key_observables: Generalization gap, pointwise Riemannian Dimension, effective dimension (d_eff), and feature compression/rank.
important_parameters: Overparameterization regime, feature-scale resolution (epsilon), and spectral decay of the feature Gram matrix.
main_assumptions: Relies on verifiable spectral conditions on learned features and assumes 1-Lipschitz activation functions where sigma(0)=0.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a pointwise generalization framework, develops a non-perturbative mathematical calculus for DNNs, establishes upper and lower bounds on the generalization gap using Riemannian Dimension, and provides empirical evidence of feature compression and optimizer bias.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
